text:
We present a new two-player online game called the ReferItGame, where two players, referred to as the “binder” and the “painter”, collaborate online to produce and verify natural language referring expressions (descriptions) that point to specific objects within real world images. Our main goal with this game is to demonstrate that by framing data creation as a collaborative work, one can potentially elicit a large amount of descriptions, with their corresponding per-instance ground truth veracities, while only needing a small portion of human rater annotations to filter out the incorrect expressions. We demonstrate the applicability of our framework by showcasing a ReferItGame-generated dataset of 130,525 referring expressions that label 96,654 unique objects shown in almost 20,000 images.

Our dataset is the first REG dataset that is substantially larger and more diverse than all previous datasets, while at the same time directly exposing the rarely studied mechanism underlying human object description in scenes. We analyze a number of aspects of the dataset, to gain understanding into how speakers choose to express objects based on category, and to what extent they describe properties such as color, size and location, as well as which names are used as initial terms for potential referents. Based on this knowledge, we formulate an optimization-based approach to REG, which not only chooses properties for mention, but also their values. Experimental evaluations on three test sets appear to confirm that our method improves upon previous approaches.
